title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
text: Imagine ’re analyzing gene expression data. might : counts matrix: genes × samples sample metadata table: sample info (treatment, batch, etc.) gene annotation table: gene symbols, descriptions, etc. three separate objects, face several problems:
code: # Dangerous: Three separate objects that can get out of sync counts <- read.csv("counts.csv", row.names = 1) sample_info <- read.csv("sample_metadata.csv") gene_info <- read.csv("gene_annotations.csv")  # What happens if you subset counts but forget to subset sample_info? counts_subset <- counts[, 1:5] # sample_info still has all samples - now mismatched!  # What if you accidentally reorder one but not the others? counts <- counts[, order(colnames(counts))] # Now sample_info doesn't match the column order!

title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
text: function creates SummarizedExperiment counts matrix metadata data.frame:
code: #' Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata #' #' @param counts A matrix of counts (genes × samples). Row names should be  #'   gene identifiers, column names should be sample identifiers. #' @param col_data A data.frame of sample metadata. Row names must match  #'   column names of counts. #' @param row_data Optional data.frame of gene metadata. Row names must match  #'   row names of counts. #' #' @return A SummarizedExperiment object #'  #' @examples #' counts <- matrix(rpois(100, 50), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) #' rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene", 1:10) #' colnames(counts) <- paste0("sample", 1:10) #' meta <- data.frame( #'   treatment = rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 5), #'   row.names = colnames(counts) #' ) #' se <- make_se(counts, meta) make_se <- function(counts, col_data, row_data = NULL) {     # Validate inputs     if (!is.matrix(counts)) {         counts <- as.matrix(counts)     }          if (!is.data.frame(col_data)) {         stop("col_data must be a data.frame")     }          # Check that sample IDs match     if (!all(colnames(counts) %in% rownames(col_data))) {         stop("Column names of counts must match row names of col_data")     }          # Reorder col_data to match counts column order     col_data <- col_data[colnames(counts), , drop = FALSE]          # Build the SummarizedExperiment     if (is.null(row_data)) {         se <- SummarizedExperiment(             assays = list(counts = counts),             colData = col_data         )     } else {         row_data <- row_data[rownames(counts), , drop = FALSE]         se <- SummarizedExperiment(             assays = list(counts = counts),             colData = col_data,             rowData = row_data         )     }          return(se) }

what: Function 2: top_variable_features() — Select Most Variable Genes
title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
text: PCA, typically want focus variable genes: Discussion: return subsetted SummarizedExperiment rather just matrix values? advantage provide?
code: #' Select top variable features #' #' @param se A SummarizedExperiment object #' @param n Number of top variable features to select (default: 500) #' @param assay_name Name of assay to use (default: "counts") #' #' @return A SummarizedExperiment subset to the top n variable features top_variable_features <- function(se, n = 500, assay_name = "counts") {     # Get the assay data     mat <- assay(se, assay_name)          # Calculate variance for each gene (row)     vars <- apply(mat, 1, var)          # Get indices of top n most variable     top_idx <- order(vars, decreasing = TRUE)[seq_len(min(n, length(vars)))]          # Subset the SummarizedExperiment     se[top_idx, ] } # Get top 500 variable genes se_top <- top_variable_features(airway, n = 500) dim(se_top)

title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
text: Now let’s run PCA data:
code: #' Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment #' #' @param se A SummarizedExperiment object #' @param assay_name Name of assay to use (default: "counts") #' @param n_top Number of top variable features to use (default: 500) #' @param scale Logical; should features be scaled? (default: TRUE) #' @param log_transform Logical; should counts be log-transformed? (default: TRUE) #' #' @return A list with: #'   - pca: The prcomp object #'   - scores: A data.frame of PC scores merged with sample metadata run_pca <- function(se, assay_name = "counts", n_top = 500,                      scale = TRUE, log_transform = TRUE) {     # Subset to top variable features     se_top <- top_variable_features(se, n = n_top, assay_name = assay_name)          # Get the data matrix     mat <- assay(se_top, assay_name)          # Log-transform if requested (add pseudocount to avoid log(0))     if (log_transform) {         mat <- log2(mat + 1)     }          # Transpose: prcomp expects samples as rows     mat_t <- t(mat)          # Run PCA     pca_result <- prcomp(mat_t, scale. = scale, center = TRUE)          # Create scores data.frame with sample metadata     scores <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)     scores$sample_id <- rownames(scores)          # Merge with colData     col_data <- as.data.frame(colData(se))     col_data$sample_id <- rownames(col_data)     scores <- merge(scores, col_data, by = "sample_id")          return(list(         pca = pca_result,         scores = scores     )) } # Run PCA on airway data pca_result <- run_pca(airway, n_top = 500)  # Examine the scores head(pca_result$scores)  # Check variance explained summary(pca_result$pca)

title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
code: #' Get variance explained by each PC #' #' @param pca_result Output from run_pca() #' #' @return A data.frame with PC names and percent variance explained pca_variance_explained <- function(pca_result) {     pca <- pca_result$pca     var_explained <- pca$sdev^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2) * 100          data.frame(         PC = paste0("PC", seq_along(var_explained)),         variance_percent = var_explained     ) } var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result) head(var_df)

title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
code: #' Create a PCA scatter plot #' #' @param pca_result Output from run_pca() #' @param color_by Column name from colData to color points by #' @param shape_by Optional column name to map to point shape #' @param pcs Which PCs to plot (default: c(1, 2)) #' @param point_size Size of points (default: 4) #' #' @return A ggplot object plot_pca <- function(pca_result, color_by = NULL, shape_by = NULL,                       pcs = c(1, 2), point_size = 4) {     scores <- pca_result$scores     var_exp <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result)          # Build PC column names     pc_x <- paste0("PC", pcs[1])     pc_y <- paste0("PC", pcs[2])          # Get variance percentages for axis labels     var_x <- round(var_exp$variance_percent[pcs[1]], 1)     var_y <- round(var_exp$variance_percent[pcs[2]], 1)          # Build the plot     p <- ggplot(scores, aes(x = .data[[pc_x]], y = .data[[pc_y]])) +         theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +         labs(             x = paste0(pc_x, " (", var_x, "% variance)"),             y = paste0(pc_y, " (", var_y, "% variance)"),             title = "PCA Plot"         )          # Add color aesthetic if specified     if (!is.null(color_by)) {         p <- p + aes(color = .data[[color_by]])     }          # Add shape aesthetic if specified     if (!is.null(shape_by)) {         p <- p + aes(shape = .data[[shape_by]])     }          # Add points     p <- p + geom_point(size = point_size)          return(p) }

what: Function 6: save_pca_results() — Export Results to Files
title: Lecture 3: Data Structures & R Ecosystems (Bioconductor)
text: CLI reproducible scripts need write analysis outputs disk. function exports PCA scores, variance, optionally plot — outputs CLI subcommand produce: Note: Every project Project Selection Guide must produce TSV files CLI. export function bridge analysis core command line.
code: #' Save PCA results to files #' #' @param pca_result Output from run_pca() #' @param output_dir Directory to save files (created if it doesn't exist) #' @param color_by Optional: metadata column for PCA plot coloring #' #' @return Invisible NULL; called for side effects (writing files) save_pca_results <- function(pca_result, output_dir, color_by = NULL) {     if (!is.character(output_dir) || length(output_dir) != 1) {         stop("output_dir must be a single directory path")     }          if (!dir.exists(output_dir)) {         dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE)     }          # Export PCA scores     scores_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_scores.tsv")     write.table(pca_result$scores, scores_file, sep = "\t",                 row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)     message("Wrote: ", scores_file)          # Export variance explained     var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result)     var_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_variance.tsv")     write.table(var_df, var_file, sep = "\t",                 row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)     message("Wrote: ", var_file)          # Optionally save the plot     if (!is.null(color_by)) {         plot_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_plot.png")         p <- plot_pca(pca_result, color_by = color_by)         ggplot2::ggsave(plot_file, p, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 150)         message("Wrote: ", plot_file)     }          invisible(NULL) } # Save results to a temporary directory tmp_out <- file.path(tempdir(), "pca_output") save_pca_results(pca_result, tmp_out, color_by = "dex")  # Check what was created list.files(tmp_out)  # Read back and verify scores_back <- read.table(file.path(tmp_out, "pca_scores.tsv"),                           header = TRUE, sep = "\t") head(scores_back)

= 14) +         labs(x = paste0(pc_x, " (", var_x, "% variance)"),              y = paste0(pc_y, " (", var_y, "% variance)"),              title = "PCA Plot")     if (!is.null(color_by)) p <- p + aes(color = .data[[color_by]])     if (!is.null(shape_by)) p <- p + aes(shape = .data[[shape_by]])     p + geom_point(size = point_size) }  #' Save PCA results to files save_pca_results <- function(pca_result, output_dir, color_by = NULL) {     if (!is.character(output_dir) || length(output_dir) != 1) {         stop("output_dir must be a single directory path")     }     if (!dir.exists(output_dir)) dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE)     scores_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_scores.tsv")     write.table(pca_result$scores, scores_file, sep = "\t",                 row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)     var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result)     var_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_variance.tsv")     write.table(var_df, var_file, sep = "\t",                 row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)     if (!is.null(color_by)) {         plot_file <- file.path(output_dir, "pca_plot.png")         p <- plot_pca(pca_result, color_by = color_by)         ggplot2::ggsave(plot_file, p, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 150)     }     invisible(NULL) }

title: Lecture 5: Lab – R Package Development (devtools)
text: Note key additions: #' @export — Makes function available users #' @param — Documents parameter #' @return — Documents return value #' @examples — Provides runnable examples pkg::fun() syntax — Explicit namespacing
code: # R/data.R  #' Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata #' #' @param counts A matrix of counts (genes × samples). Row names should be  #'   gene identifiers, column names should be sample identifiers. #' @param col_data A data.frame of sample metadata. Row names must match  #'   column names of counts. #' @param row_data Optional data.frame of gene metadata. Row names must match  #'   row names of counts. #' #' @return A SummarizedExperiment object #' @export #' #' @examples #' counts <- matrix(rpois(100, 50), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) #' rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene", 1:10) #' colnames(counts) <- paste0("sample", 1:10) #' meta <- data.frame( #'   treatment = rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 5), #'   row.names = colnames(counts) #' ) #' se <- make_se(counts, meta) make_se <- function(counts, col_data, row_data = NULL) {     if (!is.matrix(counts)) {         counts <- as.matrix(counts)     }          if (!is.data.frame(col_data)) {         stop("col_data must be a data.frame")     }          if (!all(colnames(counts) %in% rownames(col_data))) {         stop("Column names of counts must match row names of col_data")     }          col_data <- col_data[colnames(counts), , drop = FALSE]          if (is.null(row_data)) {         SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment(             assays = list(counts = counts),             colData = col_data         )     } else {         row_data <- row_data[rownames(counts), , drop = FALSE]         SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment(             assays = list(counts = counts),             colData = col_data,             rowData = row_data         )     } }  #' Select top variable features #' #' @param se A SummarizedExperiment object #' @param n Number of top variable features to select (default: 500) #' @param assay_name Name of assay to use (default: "counts") #' #' @return A SummarizedExperiment subset to the top n variable features #' @export #' #' @examples #' # Assuming 'se' is a SummarizedExperiment #' # se_top <- top_variable_features(se, n = 500) top_variable_features <- function(se, n = 500, assay_name = "counts") {     mat <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(se, assay_name)     vars <- apply(mat, 1, var)     top_idx <- order(vars, decreasing = TRUE)[seq_len(min(n, length(vars)))]     se[top_idx, ] }

code: # Create data-raw directory for data preparation scripts use_data_raw("example_se") # data-raw/example_se.R  # Create a small example SummarizedExperiment library(SummarizedExperiment)  set.seed(42)  # 100 genes, 8 samples n_genes <- 100 n_samples <- 8  # Simulate counts (negative binomial-ish) counts <- matrix(     rpois(n_genes * n_samples, lambda = 100),     nrow = n_genes,     ncol = n_samples ) rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene", seq_len(n_genes)) colnames(counts) <- paste0("sample", seq_len(n_samples))  # Add some structure: first 20 genes differ by treatment treatment <- rep(c("control", "treated"), each = 4) counts[1:20, treatment == "treated"] <- counts[1:20, treatment == "treated"] * 2  # Sample metadata sample_data <- data.frame(     sample_id = colnames(counts),     treatment = treatment,     batch = rep(c("A", "B"), times = 4),     row.names = colnames(counts) )  # Gene metadata gene_data <- data.frame(     gene_id = rownames(counts),     gene_symbol = paste0("SYM", seq_len(n_genes)),     row.names = rownames(counts) )  # Create SummarizedExperiment example_se <- SummarizedExperiment(     assays = list(counts = counts),     colData = sample_data,     rowData = gene_data )  # Save to data/ usethis::use_data(example_se, overwrite = TRUE) source("data-raw/example_se.R") use_r("data-documentation") #' Example SummarizedExperiment for testing #' #' A small SummarizedExperiment with 100 genes and 8 samples. #' Includes a treatment effect in the first 20 genes. #' #' @format A SummarizedExperiment with: #' \describe{ #'   \item{assays}{counts - raw count matrix} #'   \item{colData}{sample_id, treatment (control/treated), batch (A/B)} #'   \item{rowData}{gene_id, gene_symbol} #' } #' #' @source Simulated data for teaching purposes #' #' @examples #' data(example_se) #' example_se #' colData(example_se) "example_se"

code: use_test("pca") # tests/testthat/test-pca.R  test_that("run_pca returns correct structure", {     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")          result <- run_pca(example_se, n_top = 50)          expect_type(result, "list")     expect_named(result, c("pca", "scores"))     expect_s3_class(result$pca, "prcomp")     expect_s3_class(result$scores, "data.frame") })  test_that("run_pca scores contain sample metadata", {     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")          result <- run_pca(example_se, n_top = 50)          # Should have treatment column from colData     expect_true("treatment" %in% colnames(result$scores))     expect_true("sample_id" %in% colnames(result$scores)) })  test_that("run_pca returns expected number of PCs", {     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")          result <- run_pca(example_se, n_top = 50)          # Should have PCs equal to min(n_samples, n_features)     n_samples <- ncol(example_se)     expect_true(paste0("PC", 1) %in% colnames(result$scores))     expect_true(paste0("PC", n_samples) %in% colnames(result$scores)) })  test_that("pca_variance_explained returns percentages", {     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")          result <- run_pca(example_se, n_top = 50)     var_df <- pca_variance_explained(result)          # Variance should sum to 100     expect_equal(sum(var_df$variance_percent), 100, tolerance = 0.01)          # Should be sorted descending (PC1 explains most)     expect_true(var_df$variance_percent[1] >= var_df$variance_percent[2]) })

code: use_r("export") # R/export.R  #' Save PCA results to files #' #' @param pca_result Output from run_pca() #' @param output_dir Directory to save files #' @param prefix Prefix for filenames (default: "pca") #' #' @return Invisible NULL; files are written to output_dir #' @export #' #' @examples #' \dontrun{ #' result <- run_pca(se) #' save_pca_results(result, "output/") #' } save_pca_results <- function(pca_result, output_dir, prefix = "pca") {     if (!dir.exists(output_dir)) {         dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE)     }          # Save scores     scores_file <- file.path(output_dir, paste0(prefix, "_scores.tsv"))     utils::write.table(         pca_result$scores,         scores_file,         sep = "\t",         row.names = FALSE,         quote = FALSE     )          # Save variance explained     var_file <- file.path(output_dir, paste0(prefix, "_variance.tsv"))     var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result)     utils::write.table(         var_df,         var_file,         sep = "\t",         row.names = FALSE,         quote = FALSE     )          message("Saved: ", scores_file)     message("Saved: ", var_file)          invisible(NULL) }

code: use_test("export") # tests/testthat/test-export.R  test_that("save_pca_results creates files", {     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")     result <- run_pca(example_se, n_top = 50)          # Use a temporary directory     tmp_dir <- tempdir()     output_dir <- file.path(tmp_dir, "test_output")          save_pca_results(result, output_dir, prefix = "test")          # Check files exist     expect_true(file.exists(file.path(output_dir, "test_scores.tsv")))     expect_true(file.exists(file.path(output_dir, "test_variance.tsv")))          # Check scores file has correct structure     scores <- read.table(         file.path(output_dir, "test_scores.tsv"),         header = TRUE,         sep = "\t"     )     expect_true("PC1" %in% colnames(scores))     expect_true("sample_id" %in% colnames(scores))          # Clean up     unlink(output_dir, recursive = TRUE) })

title: Lecture 7: Lab - Shiny (R) - Reactivity and App Design
text: Shiny uses reactivity automatically update outputs inputs change: Sources: Inputs change (input$n, reactive values) Conductors: Computations depend sources (reactive(), renderX) Endpoints: Outputs display results (output$X) source changes, Shiny automatically reruns dependent code.

title: Lecture 7: Lab - Shiny (R) - Reactivity and App Design
text: Let’s build PCA explorer step step. First, minimal version: Save app.R run !
code: library(shiny) library(sePCA) library(airway)  # Pre-load data data(airway)  ui <- fluidPage(     theme = bslib::bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"),          titlePanel("PCA Explorer"),          sidebarLayout(         sidebarPanel(             h4("Settings"),             numericInput(                 "n_top",                 "Number of top variable genes:",                 value = 500,                 min = 50,                 max = 5000,                 step = 50             ),             selectInput(                 "color_by",                 "Color by:",                 choices = c("dex", "cell")             )         ),         mainPanel(             h4("PCA Plot"),             plotOutput("pca_plot", height = "500px")         )     ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {     output$pca_plot <- renderPlot({         # Run PCA with user-selected parameters         result <- run_pca(airway, n_top = input$n_top)                  # Create plot         plot_pca(result, color_by = input$color_by)     }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Lecture 7: Lab - Shiny (R) - Reactivity and App Design
text: app problem: every time change color_by, re-runs entire PCA! ’s wasteful — PCA depends n_top. Solution: Use reactive() cache expensive computations: Now reactive graph looks like: Changing n_top → reruns pca_result() → reruns plot Changing color_by → reruns plot (uses cached PCA) Exercise : Add second selectInput shape_by update plot use aesthetics. Verify changing shape doesn’t re-run PCA.
code: server <- function(input, output, session) {          # Reactive expression: only reruns when n_top changes     pca_result <- reactive({         run_pca(airway, n_top = input$n_top)     })          output$pca_plot <- renderPlot({         # Use the cached result         plot_pca(pca_result(), color_by = input$color_by)     }) } input$n_top ──→ pca_result() ──→ renderPlot() ──→ output$pca_plot                                        ↑ input$color_by ────────────────────────┘

title: Lecture 7: Lab - Shiny (R) - Reactivity and App Design
text: Let’s add user controls:
code: ui <- fluidPage(     theme = bslib::bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"),          titlePanel("PCA Explorer"),          sidebarLayout(         sidebarPanel(             h4("PCA Settings"),             numericInput(                 "n_top",                 "Top variable genes:",                 value = 500, min = 50, max = 5000, step = 50             ),             checkboxInput(                 "log_transform",                 "Log-transform counts",                 value = TRUE             ),             checkboxInput(                 "scale",                 "Scale features",                 value = TRUE             ),                          hr(),             h4("Plot Settings"),                          selectInput("color_by", "Color by:", choices = c("dex", "cell")),             selectInput("shape_by", "Shape by:", choices = c("None", "dex", "cell")),                          fluidRow(                 column(6, numericInput("pc_x", "PC X:", value = 1, min = 1, max = 8)),                 column(6, numericInput("pc_y", "PC Y:", value = 2, min = 1, max = 8))             ),                          sliderInput(                 "point_size",                 "Point size:",                 value = 4, min = 1, max = 10             )         ),                  mainPanel(             tabsetPanel(                 tabPanel(                     "PCA Plot",                     plotOutput("pca_plot", height = "500px")                 ),                 tabPanel(                     "Variance Explained",                     plotOutput("variance_plot", height = "400px")                 ),                 tabPanel(                     "Sample Data",                     DT::dataTableOutput("scores_table")                 )             )         )     ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {          # Cached PCA result     pca_result <- reactive({         run_pca(             airway,             n_top = input$n_top,             log_transform = input$log_transform,             scale = input$scale         )     })          # PCA scatter plot     output$pca_plot <- renderPlot({         shape <- if (input$shape_by == "None") NULL else input$shape_by                  plot_pca(             pca_result(),             color_by = input$color_by,             shape_by = shape,             pcs = c(input$pc_x, input$pc_y),             point_size = input$point_size         )     })          # Variance plot     output$variance_plot <- renderPlot({         var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result())                  # Only show first 8 PCs         var_df <- var_df[1:min(8, nrow(var_df)), ]         var_df$PC <- factor(var_df$PC, levels = var_df$PC)                  ggplot2::ggplot(var_df, ggplot2::aes(x = PC, y = variance_percent)) +             ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +             ggplot2::geom_text(                 ggplot2::aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", variance_percent)),                 vjust = -0.5             ) +             ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +             ggplot2::labs(                 x = "Principal Component",                 y = "Variance Explained (%)",                 title = "Variance Explained by Each PC"             ) +             ggplot2::ylim(0, max(var_df$variance_percent) * 1.1)     })          # Scores table     output$scores_table <- DT::renderDataTable({         pca_result()$scores     }, options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE)) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

code: server <- function(input, output, session) {     pca_result <- reactive({         # Validate inputs before computing         validate(             need(input$n_top >= 10, "Please select at least 10 genes"),             need(input$n_top <= nrow(airway), "Cannot select more genes than available")         )                  run_pca(airway, n_top = input$n_top)     })          output$pca_plot <- renderPlot({         # Validate PC selection         n_samples <- ncol(airway)         validate(             need(input$pc_x <= n_samples, paste("PC X must be ≤", n_samples)),             need(input$pc_y <= n_samples, paste("PC Y must be ≤", n_samples)),             need(input$pc_x != input$pc_y, "Please select different PCs for X and Y")         )                  plot_pca(             pca_result(),             color_by = input$color_by,             pcs = c(input$pc_x, input$pc_y)         )     }) }

code: # R/app_server.R  #' Shiny App Server #' #' @param input Shiny input #' @param output Shiny output #' @param session Shiny session #' #' @return NULL (side effects only) #' @noRd #' #' @importFrom SummarizedExperiment colData #' @importFrom ggplot2 ggplot aes geom_col geom_text theme_minimal labs ylim #' @importFrom rlang .data app_server <- function(input, output, session) {     # Load example data     # In a real app, you might want to let users upload their own data     se_data <- shiny::reactive({         data("airway", package = "airway", envir = environment())         get("airway", envir = environment())     })          # Update select inputs based on available metadata     shiny::observe({         se <- se_data()         cols <- colnames(SummarizedExperiment::colData(se))         shiny::updateSelectInput(session, "color_by", choices = cols)         shiny::updateSelectInput(session, "shape_by", choices = c("None", cols))     })          # Compute PCA     pca_result <- shiny::reactive({         shiny::req(se_data(), input$n_top)                  run_pca(             se_data(),             n_top = input$n_top,             log_transform = input$log_transform,             scale = input$scale         )     })          # PCA scatter plot     output$pca_plot <- shiny::renderPlot({         shiny::req(pca_result(), input$color_by)                  shape <- if (is.null(input$shape_by) || input$shape_by == "None") {             NULL         } else {             input$shape_by         }                  plot_pca(             pca_result(),             color_by = input$color_by,             shape_by = shape,             point_size = input$point_size         )     })          # Variance plot     output$variance_plot <- shiny::renderPlot({         shiny::req(pca_result())                  var_df <- pca_variance_explained(pca_result())         var_df <- var_df[seq_len(min(8, nrow(var_df))), ]         var_df$PC <- factor(var_df$PC, levels = var_df$PC)                  ggplot2::ggplot(var_df, ggplot2::aes(x = .data$PC, y = .data$variance_percent)) +             ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +             ggplot2::geom_text(                 ggplot2::aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", .data$variance_percent)),                 vjust = -0.5, size = 4             ) +             ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +             ggplot2::labs(                 x = "Principal Component",                 y = "Variance Explained (%)"             ) +             ggplot2::ylim(0, max(var_df$variance_percent) * 1.15)     })          # Scores table     output$scores_table <- DT::renderDataTable({         shiny::req(pca_result())         DT::datatable(             pca_result()$scores,             options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE)         )     })          # Download handler     output$download_plot <- shiny::downloadHandler(         filename = function() {             paste0("pca_plot_", Sys.Date(), ".png")         },         content = function(file) {             shape <- if (is.null(input$shape_by) || input$shape_by == "None") {                 NULL             } else {                 input$shape_by             }                          p <- plot_pca(                 pca_result(),                 color_by = input$color_by,                 shape_by = shape,                 point_size = input$point_size             )                          ggplot2::ggsave(file, p, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 150)         }     ) }

text: Rapp infers CLI structure normal R code: n_top <- 500L becomes --n-top 500 log_transform <- TRUE becomes --log-transform / ---log-transform path <- NULL becomes positional argument switch("", cmd1 = { ... }, cmd2 = { ... }) declares commands can add help metadata using #| annotations: Note: snake_case names automatically map kebab-case flags (n_top -> --n-top). Rapp provides built-help: --help shows usage options --help-yaml prints machine-readable metadata

title: Lecture 9: Lab – CLI Tool Development (R) – CLI Design and Rapp Basics
text: Create exec/sePCA:
code: #!/usr/bin/env Rapp #| name: sePCA #| title: sePCA CLI #| description: PCA analysis for SummarizedExperiment data.  suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(sePCA))  # Helper to read TSV/CSV read_data_file <- function(path) {     ext <- tolower(tools::file_ext(path))     if (ext == "csv") {         read.csv(path, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)     } else {         read.table(path, sep = "\t", header = TRUE, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)     } }  switch(     "",      #| title: Run PCA analysis     #| description: Run PCA and export results.     pca = {         #| description: Path to counts matrix (TSV/CSV)         #| short: c         counts <- ""          #| description: Path to sample metadata (TSV/CSV)         #| short: m         meta <- ""          #| description: Output directory         #| short: o         output <- ""          #| description: Number of top variable genes         #| short: n         n_top <- 500L          #| description: Log-transform counts         log_transform <- TRUE          #| description: Metadata column for plot coloring (optional)         color_by <- ""          if (counts == "" || meta == "" || output == "") {             stop("--counts, --meta, and --output are required", call. = FALSE)         }         if (!file.exists(counts)) stop("File not found: ", counts, call. = FALSE)         if (!file.exists(meta)) stop("File not found: ", meta, call. = FALSE)          if (!dir.exists(output)) dir.create(output, recursive = TRUE)          counts_df <- read_data_file(counts)         meta_df <- read_data_file(meta)         se <- make_se(as.matrix(counts_df), meta_df)          result <- run_pca(se, n_top = n_top, log_transform = log_transform)          scores_file <- file.path(output, "pca_scores.tsv")         write.table(result$scores, scores_file, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)          var_file <- file.path(output, "pca_variance.tsv")         var_df <- pca_variance_explained(result)         write.table(var_df, var_file, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)          if (color_by != "") {             plot_file <- file.path(output, "pca_plot.png")             p <- plot_pca(result, color_by = color_by)             ggplot2::ggsave(plot_file, p, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 150)         }     },      #| title: Validate input files     #| description: Check that inputs exist, parse, and report dimensions.     validate = {         #| description: Path to counts matrix (TSV/CSV)         counts <- ""          #| description: Path to sample metadata (TSV/CSV)         meta <- ""          if (counts == "" || meta == "") {             stop("--counts and --meta are required", call. = FALSE)         }         if (!file.exists(counts)) stop("File not found: ", counts, call. = FALSE)         if (!file.exists(meta)) stop("File not found: ", meta, call. = FALSE)          counts_df <- read_data_file(counts)         meta_df <- read_data_file(meta)          message("Counts dimensions: ", nrow(counts_df), " genes x ", ncol(counts_df), " samples")         message("Metadata rows: ", nrow(meta_df))          if (!all(colnames(counts_df) %in% rownames(meta_df))) {             stop("Sample IDs in counts do not match metadata row names", call. = FALSE)         }          message("Inputs look valid.")     } )

text: Add CLI section:
code: ## Command Line Interface  sePCA includes a command-line interface for pipeline integration.  ### Quick Start  ```bash # Install the launcher (one-time) Rscript -e "Rapp::install_pkg_cli_apps('sePCA')"  # Run PCA analysis sePCA pca \     --counts counts.tsv \     --meta samples.tsv \     --output results/ \     --n-top 500 \     --color-by treatment ```  ### Fallback (No Launcher)  ```bash Rapp exec/sePCA pca --help ```  ### Available Commands  | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `pca` | Run PCA analysis and export results | | `validate` | Validate input files |  Run `sePCA <command> --help` for detailed options.

title: Lecture 10: Lab – CLI Tool Development (R) – Packaging and Installation
text: Create vignettes/cli.Rmd:
code: --- title: "Command Line Interface" output: rmarkdown::html_vignette vignette: >   %\VignetteIndexEntry{Command Line Interface}   %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}   %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8} ---  ## Overview  The sePCA CLI allows you to run PCA analysis from the command line, making it easy to integrate into pipelines.  ## Installation  After installing sePCA, install the CLI launcher:  ```r remotes::install_github("you/sePCA") Rapp::install_pkg_cli_apps("sePCA") ```  ## Usage  ### Basic PCA Analysis  ```bash sePCA pca \     --counts counts.tsv \     --meta samples.tsv \     --output results/ ```  ### Input File Formats  **Counts matrix** (`--counts`): - Tab-separated or comma-separated - First column: gene IDs (row names) - Subsequent columns: sample counts - Header row with sample IDs  ``` gene_id    sample1    sample2    sample3 ENSG001    100        150        120 ENSG002    50         75         60 ```  **Metadata** (`--meta`): - Tab-separated or comma-separated - First column: sample IDs (must match counts columns) - Additional columns: sample attributes  ``` sample_id    treatment    batch sample1      control      A sample2      treated      A sample3      treated      B ```  ### Output Files  | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `pca_scores.tsv` | PCA scores merged with sample metadata | | `pca_variance.tsv` | Variance explained by each PC | | `pca_plot.png` | PCA scatter plot (if `--color-by` specified) |  ### Full Options  ``` sePCA pca --help ```  ## Pipeline Integration  ### Snakemake  ```python rule pca:     input:         counts = "data/{sample}/counts.tsv",         meta = "data/{sample}/meta.tsv"     output:         directory("results/{sample}/pca/")     shell:         """         sePCA pca \             --counts {input.counts} \             --meta {input.meta} \             --output {output}         """ ```  ### Bash Loop  ```bash for dataset in data/*; do     name=$(basename "$dataset")     sePCA pca \         --counts "$dataset/counts.tsv" \         --meta "$dataset/meta.tsv" \         --output "results/$name/" done ```

text: Create tests/testthat/test-cli.R:
code: test_that("CLI produces same output as R functions", {     skip_on_cran()  # CLI tests may be slow          # Create temp files     tmp_dir <- tempdir()     counts_file <- file.path(tmp_dir, "counts.tsv")     meta_file <- file.path(tmp_dir, "meta.tsv")     output_dir <- file.path(tmp_dir, "cli_output")          # Create test data     data(example_se, package = "sePCA")     counts <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(example_se, "counts")     meta <- as.data.frame(SummarizedExperiment::colData(example_se))          write.table(counts, counts_file, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)     write.table(meta, meta_file, sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)          # Run via R     se <- make_se(counts, meta)     r_result <- run_pca(se, n_top = 50)          # Run via CLI     Rapp::run(system.file("exec", "sePCA", package = "sePCA"), c(         "pca",         "--counts", counts_file,         "--meta", meta_file,         "--output", output_dir,         "--n-top", "50"     ))          # Load CLI output     cli_scores <- read.table(         file.path(output_dir, "pca_scores.tsv"),         header = TRUE,         sep = "\t"     )          # Compare dimensions     expect_equal(nrow(cli_scores), nrow(r_result$scores))     expect_equal(ncol(cli_scores), ncol(r_result$scores))          # Compare PC values (within tolerance)     expect_equal(cli_scores$PC1, r_result$scores$PC1, tolerance = 1e-10)          # Cleanup     unlink(output_dir, recursive = TRUE) })

They file one constructive GitHub issue:    - Be specific about what doesn't work    - Include exact error messages    - Suggest a fix if you can  ---  # Final Submission Checklist  ## Before You Submit  ```markdown ## HW1 Submission Checklist  ### Package Basics - [ ] Package installs from GitHub without errors - [ ] `devtools::check()` passes with 0 errors, 0 warnings - [ ] All exported functions have roxygen2 documentation - [ ] README exists and renders on GitHub  ### Core Functionality - [ ] `make_se()` creates a valid SummarizedExperiment - [ ] `run_pca()` returns list with `pca` and `scores` - [ ] `plot_pca()` returns a ggplot object  ### Testing - [ ] At least 5 test expectations across 2+ files - [ ] All tests pass with `devtools::test()` - [ ] CI runs on GitHub (Actions badge shows)  ### Shiny App - [ ] `run_app()` launches without errors - [ ] Plot updates when inputs change - [ ] App can be run from fresh R session  ### CLI - [ ] `exec/sePCA` exists in the package - [ ] `Rapp::install_pkg_cli_apps("sePCA")` installs a launcher - [ ] `--help` shows usage information - [ ] PCA command produces output files  ### Documentation - [ ] pkgdown site builds successfully - [ ] At least one vignette/article exists - [ ] README has installation and quick start  ### Final Verification - [ ] From fresh R: `remotes::install_github("user/repo")` works - [ ] From fresh R: `library(pkg); run_app()` works - [ ] From terminal: CLI produces expected outputs

title: Getting Started with ADS 8192
text: package organized around principle shows virtually every well-engineered codebase: separate compute users access . analysis logic lives small, testable, composable R functions (core layer). Presentation layers — R API, Shiny app, CLI — thin wrappers delegate core functions. every interface calls core functions, get several properties free: DRY (Don’t Repeat ) — fix bug add feature , every interface benefits immediately. Testability — core functions pure R well-defined inputs outputs, unit tests cover real computation regardless ’s invoked. Composability — function one thing, users (packages) can recombine ways didn’t anticipate. layered design package — course lectures — structured way : build core functions first (Lecture 03), package test (Lectures 05–06), add presentation layers one time (Shiny 07–08, CLI 09–10).
code: Core Analysis Functions   make_se() → run_pca() → plot_pca()         ↑            ↑            ↑    ┌────┴────┐  ┌────┴────┐  ┌───┴─────┐    │ R API   │  │ Shiny   │  │  CLI    │    │ (users) │  │ (web)   │  │(scripts)│    └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Homework 1, student builds complete R package implements bioinformatics analysis using “three interfaces, one core” architecture taught class: R API — exported functions roxygen2 documentation Shiny app — interactive exploration via run_app() CLI — command-line interface via Rapp exec/ projects share rubric (25 points) structural requirements. differs core analysis dataset. Choose one 13 projects , propose (see Custom Projects end). project uses different Bioconductor dataset chosen biologically appropriate analysis. Depending project, primary data structure either SummarizedExperiment (bulk experiments) SingleCellExperiment (single-cell experiments). package must include: constructor function (make_se() make_sce()) build appropriate container raw inputs 3–5 additional exported analysis/visualization functions least 8 testthat expectations across 2+ test files pkgdown documentation site TSV file exports via CLI bundled example dataset data/ generated via data-raw/ script

what: How to Read Each Project Description
title: Project Selection Guide
text: shared stack every project uses: ggplot2, rlang, shiny, bslib, DT, Rapp, testthat, roxygen2, pkgdown, plus either SummarizedExperiment SingleCellExperiment ().

title: Project Selection Guide
text: course example. may choose project — provided reference structure style. Package name: ADS8192 (repo) Rationale: PCA common first step exploratory analysis high-dimensional data. reduces thousands features principal components capture dominant sources variation dataset.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: airway package contains RNA-seq experiment human airway smooth muscle cell lines treated dexamethasone (glucocorticoid). 8 samples, 4 treated 4 untreated, ~64,000 genes. Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment counts matrix sample metadata Select variable genes dataset Run PCA filtered, optionally transformed expression data Summarize variance explained per principal component Visualize samples PC space, colored shaped metadata columns Export PCA scores variance summaries TSV files Key parameters: n_top, log_transform, scale, color_by, shape_by CLI outputs: pca_scores.tsv, pca_variance.tsv Shiny inputs: Number top genes, log-transform toggle, scale toggle, PC axes, color/shape metadata column, point size
code: BiocManager::install("airway") library(airway) library(SummarizedExperiment)  data("airway")

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: UMAP nonlinear dimensionality reduction method reveals biological structure — cell types — PCA often misses. standard visualization single-cell RNA-seq analysis, tuning parameters (n_neighbors, min_dist) meaningfully changes result.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Zeisel et al. (2015) dataset profiles 3,005 cells mouse somatosensory cortex hippocampus, classified 7 major cell types (interneurons, pyramidal neurons, oligodendrocytes, microglia, astrocytes, endothelial, mural cells). clear cell-type structure makes UMAP parameters visually interpretable. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Select variable genes downstream analysis Run UMAP dimensionality reduction configurable parameters (neighbors, min_dist, metric) Summarize embedding (parameter values, spread statistics) Visualize cells UMAP space, colored/shaped metadata columns Export UMAP coordinates parameter summaries TSV files Key parameters: n_top, n_neighbors, min_dist, metric (euclidean/cosine), color_by, shape_by CLI outputs: umap_embeddings.tsv (cell × UMAP1/UMAP2 + metadata), umap_params.tsv (parameter values + spread stats) Shiny inputs: n_top slider, n_neighbors slider (5–50), min_dist slider (0.01–1.0), metric dropdown, color/shape metadata Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, uwot, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- ZeiselBrainData() example_sce <- sce  # Keep only counts + key metadata assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, c("level1class", "level2class")] colnames(colData(example_sce)) <- c("cell_type", "cell_subtype")  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: formal analysis, must check whether samples group expected biological variable (treatment, genotype) rather technical artifacts (batch, patient). sample--sample correlation heatmap hierarchical clustering standard first diagnostic.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: parathyroidSE package contains RNA-seq data 27 samples human parathyroid adenoma tissue: 3 patients × 3 treatments (Control, DPN, OHT) × time points. dual grouping patient treatment creates interesting similarity structure biological individual effects compete. Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment counts matrix sample metadata Select variable genes Compute pairwise sample similarity (correlation distance matrix) Perform hierarchical clustering, cut chosen k, compute silhouette widths assess cluster quality Visualize annotated heatmap dendrogram sidebars metadata color bars Export similarity matrix cluster assignments TSV files Key parameters: n_top, method (pearson/spearman), linkage (ward.D2/complete/average), k CLI outputs: similarity_matrix.tsv, cluster_assignments.tsv Shiny inputs: Correlation method, linkage method, k slider, n_top slider, metadata columns annotation bars Dependencies: SummarizedExperiment, parathyroidSE (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_se.R BiocManager::install("parathyroidSE") library(parathyroidSE) library(SummarizedExperiment)  data("parathyroidGenesSE") se <- parathyroidGenesSE  # Subset to top 2000 genes by variance for package size counts <- assay(se) vars <- apply(counts, 1, var) keep_genes <- names(sort(vars, decreasing = TRUE))[1:2000] example_se <- se[keep_genes, ]  # Simplify colData colData(example_se) <- colData(example_se)[, c("treatment", "patient", "time")]  usethis::use_data(example_se, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: central question gene expression experiments: genes change conditions? Per-gene statistical testing fold-change estimation, followed volcano MA plot visualization, foundational analysis transcriptomics.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Bottomly et al. (2011) dataset contains bulk RNA-seq data two inbred mouse strains — C57BL/6J DBA/2J — 10 11 biological replicates respectively (21 samples total). two strains well-characterized gene expression differences, making clean, real-world dataset demonstrating per-gene differential expression testing. balanced design moderate sample size ideal t-test Wilcoxon approaches. Available via recount3 (project SRP001540). Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment counts matrix sample metadata Filter genes low expression across samples Run per-gene statistical testing (t-test Wilcoxon) two groups, computing log2 fold changes, p-values, BH-adjusted p-values Summarize number significantly //non-significant genes given thresholds Visualize results volcano plot (log2FC vs. −log10 p) MA plot (average expression vs. log2FC), significant genes highlighted Export differential expression results summary counts TSV files Key parameters: group_column, ref_level, fc_threshold, p_threshold, test_method (t.test/wilcox), plot_type (volcano/ma) CLI outputs: de_results.tsv, de_summary.tsv Shiny inputs: Grouping column, reference level, FC threshold slider, p-value threshold slider, test method toggle, volcano ↔︎ MA radio button Dependencies: SummarizedExperiment, recount3 (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_se.R BiocManager::install("recount3") library(recount3) library(SummarizedExperiment)  # Access the Bottomly et al. dataset via recount3 human_projects <- available_projects() proj <- subset(human_projects, file_source == "sra" &                  project == "SRP001540") rse <- create_rse(proj)  # Transform raw counts assay(rse, "counts") <- transform_counts(rse)  # Simplify colData - extract strain from attributes example_se <- rse cd <- colData(example_se) strain <- ifelse(grepl("C57BL/6J", cd$sra.sample_attributes),                  "C57BL_6J", "DBA_2J") colData(example_se) <- DataFrame(   sample_id = colnames(example_se),   strain = factor(strain) )  usethis::use_data(example_se, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: K-means clustering partitions cells k groups minimizing within-cluster variance. Choosing k real practical decision — elbow plot within-cluster sum squares standard diagnostic. Single-cell data many unlabeled cell types natural application.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Baron et al. (2016) dataset profiles ~8,500 cells human pancreatic islets, capturing 14 cell types including alpha, beta, delta, gamma, acinar, ductal cells. Students must discover “right” number clusters data alone — elbow silhouette diagnostics suggest k ≈ 8–14, matching known biology. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Select variable genes Run k-means clustering across range k values (2..max_k) Compute clustering quality metrics (total within-cluster sum squares, average silhouette width) k Visualize clusters PCA projection, colored cluster assignment Generate elbow plot (WSS vs. k) choosing optimal number clusters Export cluster assignments quality metrics TSV files Key parameters: n_top, max_k, n_starts, selected_k CLI outputs: cluster_assignments.tsv, kmeans_metrics.tsv Shiny inputs: n_top slider, max_k slider, selected k slider, toggle elbow plot cluster scatter, color cluster cell_type metadata Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, cluster, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- BaronPancreasData("human") example_sce <- sce  assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, "label", drop = FALSE] colnames(colData(example_sce)) <- "cell_type"  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Quality control essential first step single-cell analysis. biological interpretation, low-quality cells — detected genes, low library sizes, high spike-proportions — must identified flagged. ERCC spike-controls provide ground-truth reference QC.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Lun et al. (2017) dataset contains 192 mouse cells ERCC spike-controls. specifically designed benchmarking normalization QC methods. spike-proportions library size variation create natural QC outliers students must detect. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Compute per-cell QC metrics: library size, number genes detected, spike-percentage, Shannon entropy Flag outlier cells using MAD-based thresholds QC metrics Visualize QC results multi-panel display: library size distribution, genes vs. library size scatter, spike-percentage histogram, outliers highlighted Export QC metrics pass/fail flags TSV files Key parameters: mad_threshold, min_genes, min_library_size, max_spike_pct CLI outputs: qc_metrics.tsv, qc_flags.tsv Shiny inputs: MAD threshold slider, minimum genes input, max spike-% slider; panels update reflect pass/fail counts Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- LunSpikeInData() example_sce <- sce  # Mark ERCC spike-ins in rowData is_spike <- grepl("^ERCC-", rownames(example_sce)) rowData(example_sce)$is_spike <- is_spike  # Keep key metadata colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, c("cell_line", "plate")]  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Identifying highly variable genes (HVGs) critical feature selection step dimensionality reduction clustering. mean-variance relationship count data non-trivial — high-mean genes high variance default. Understanding visualizing relationship teaches students naive variance ranking misleading methods like loess trend correction exist.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Macosko et al. (2015) Drop-seq dataset profiles ~44,000 cells mouse retina, spanning ~40 cell clusters including rods, cones, bipolar cells, amacrine cells, ganglion cells. enormous cell-type diversity means HVG selection critically determines downstream structure recovered — making ideal dataset exploring variance-based feature selection. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Filter genes minimum mean expression threshold Compute per-gene statistics: mean, variance, coefficient variation, dispersion, ranking Summarize number highly variable genes different thresholds fit mean-variance trend Visualize mean-variance relationship scatter plot loess trend line, highlighting selected HVGs Export gene statistics HVG list TSV files Key parameters: min_mean, variance_threshold, n_top_genes CLI outputs: gene_statistics.tsv, hvg_list.tsv Shiny inputs: Minimum mean slider, variance threshold n_top input, scatter interactive HVG highlighting, linked gene table Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- MacoskoRetinaData() set.seed(42) # Subsample to 10000 cells for package size (full dataset has ~44k cells) keep_cells <- sample(ncol(sce), 10000) example_sce <- sce[, keep_cells]  assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) # Cluster labels from the original paper colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, "cluster", drop = FALSE]  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Rather analyzing genes individually, biologists often ask whether predefined set genes (pathway, signature) collectively - -regulated. Per-sample gene set scores reduce pathway single number per sample, enabling group comparisons. project combines expression data external pathway knowledge MSigDB.

what: Dataset: TCGA BRCA (Human bulk RNA-seq) + MSigDB Hallmark Gene Sets
title: Project Selection Guide
text: curatedTCGAData package provides access Cancer Genome Atlas data. BRCA (breast cancer) cohort includes RNA-seq expression data ~1,200 patients rich clinical annotations (ER/PR/HER2 status, PAM50 subtype, stage). Immune proliferation-related gene sets MSigDB clearly distinguish molecular subtypes — making pathway-level scoring biologically compelling. subset manageable number samples example data. Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment expression matrix sample metadata Read gene sets GMT file named list character vectors Compute per-sample gene set scores (e.g., mean z-score set members) Summarize mean scores per group compute effect sizes conditions Visualize gene set scores boxplots violin plots, grouped clinical variable faceted gene set Export per-sample scores group summaries TSV files Key parameters: gene_sets (list GMT path), score_method (mean_z, median_z), group_column CLI outputs: geneset_scores.tsv, scoring_summary.tsv Shiny inputs: Select built-sets upload GMT, score method dropdown, grouping variable dropdown (ER status, PAM50 subtype), gene set selector, boxplot ↔︎ violin toggle Dependencies: SummarizedExperiment, curatedTCGAData (data ), msigdbr (gene sets)
code: ## data-raw/example_se.R BiocManager::install(c("curatedTCGAData", "TCGAutils")) install.packages("msigdbr") library(curatedTCGAData) library(TCGAutils) library(SummarizedExperiment)  # Download BRCA RNA-seq data brca <- curatedTCGAData("BRCA", "RNASeq2*", version = "2.0.1",                          dry.run = FALSE) rse <- experiments(brca)[[1]]  # Subset to 200 samples for package size set.seed(42) keep_samples <- sample(ncol(rse), 200) rse <- rse[, keep_samples]  # Subset to top 5000 variable genes vars <- apply(assay(rse), 1, var, na.rm = TRUE) keep_genes <- names(sort(vars, decreasing = TRUE))[1:5000] rse <- rse[keep_genes, ]  # Get clinical data clin <- colData(brca) clin <- clin[colnames(rse), ]  example_se <- SummarizedExperiment(   assays = list(exprs = assay(rse)),   colData = DataFrame(     sample_id   = colnames(rse),     er_status   = clin$pathologic_stage,     pam50       = clin$subtype_PAM50.mRNA   ) )  # Bundle a small set of hallmark gene sets library(msigdbr) hallmarks <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", category = "H") example_gene_sets <- split(hallmarks$gene_symbol, hallmarks$gs_name) # Keep 10 immune/proliferation-relevant sets keep_sets <- c(   "HALLMARK_IL2_STAT5_SIGNALING", "HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE",   "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE", "HALLMARK_ALLOGRAFT_REJECTION",   "HALLMARK_MYC_TARGETS_V1", "HALLMARK_E2F_TARGETS",   "HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB", "HALLMARK_APOPTOSIS",   "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY", "HALLMARK_COMPLEMENT" ) example_gene_sets <- example_gene_sets[keep_sets]  usethis::use_data(example_se, example_gene_sets, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Raw counts must normalized cross-sample comparison. Different methods (CPM, log2-CPM, quantile, upper-quartile) make different assumptions. Visualizing method reshapes per-sample distributions teaches students normalization matters — “right” method depends data.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Muraro et al. (2016) dataset scRNAseq profiles ~2,300 cells human donor pancreatic islets using CEL-Seq2. Cell types include alpha, beta, delta, acinar, ductal, . dramatic library size variation across cells (typical scRNA-seq) makes normalization effects visually striking — CPM, log2-CPM, quantile normalization reshape per-cell distributions different ways. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Filter low-expression genes Apply multiple normalization methods (CPM, log2-CPM, quantile) store normalized values new assays SCE Compute per-cell distributional statistics (median, IQR, CV) normalization method Visualize side--side distributions across methods boxplots density curves Export normalized counts normalization statistics TSV files Key parameters: methods (character vector: “cpm”, “log2”, “quantile”), min_count CLI outputs: normalized_counts.tsv, normalization_stats.tsv Shiny inputs: Checkbox group select methods, boxplot ↔︎ density toggle, per-cell per-method view Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- MuraroPancreasData() example_sce <- sce  assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, "label", drop = FALSE] colnames(colData(example_sce)) <- "cell_type"  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Co-expressed genes often share biological functions. Building gene-gene correlation network — computing pairwise correlations, thresholding adjacency matrix, identifying hub genes — lightweight introduction network biology. Cohorts many samples provide stable pairwise gene correlations reveal known co-regulation patterns.

what: Dataset: GTEx Skeletal Muscle (Human bulk RNA-seq)
title: Project Selection Guide
text: recount3 package provides access GTEx (Genotype-Tissue Expression) project. skeletal muscle tissue one largest sample sizes GTEx (~800 samples), providing highly stable gene-gene correlations. Known co-expression modules — mitochondrial respiration genes, sarcomere components, extracellular matrix genes — recoverable, making biologically rich dataset network analysis. Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment counts matrix sample metadata Select variable genes network construction Compute pairwise gene-gene correlation matrix Threshold correlation matrix binary adjacency matrix compute per-gene network statistics (degree, betweenness centrality) Summarize network: total edge count, mean degree, hub genes connectivity threshold Visualize network correlation heatmap gene clustering, force-directed layout Export correlations, network summary, hub gene lists TSV files Key parameters: n_top, cor_method (pearson/spearman), cor_threshold CLI outputs: gene_correlations.tsv, network_summary.tsv, hub_genes.tsv Shiny inputs: Correlation threshold slider, method dropdown, n_top slider, hub gene table, heatmap redraws threshold change Dependencies: SummarizedExperiment, igraph (Suggests, force-directed layout), recount3 (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_se.R BiocManager::install("recount3") library(recount3) library(SummarizedExperiment)  # Access GTEx skeletal muscle data human_projects <- available_projects() proj <- subset(human_projects, file_source == "gtex" &                  project == "MUSCLE") rse <- create_rse(proj)  # Transform raw counts assay(rse, "counts") <- transform_counts(rse)  # Subset to top 2000 genes by variance for tractable network computation vars <- apply(assay(rse, "counts"), 1, var) keep_genes <- names(sort(vars, decreasing = TRUE))[1:2000]  # Subset to 200 samples for package size set.seed(42) keep_samples <- sample(ncol(rse), min(200, ncol(rse)))  example_se <- rse[keep_genes, keep_samples] assays(example_se) <- list(counts = assay(example_se, "counts")) colData(example_se) <- colData(example_se)[, c("gtex.age", "gtex.sex"),                                             drop = FALSE]  usethis::use_data(example_se, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: genes--samples heatmap one iconic figures genomics. Building one scratch — row scaling, hierarchical clustering axes, clinical annotation bars — requires integrating several analysis steps single publication-quality visualization.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: curatedTCGAData package provides RNA-seq expression data Cancer Genome Atlas. GBM (glioblastoma multiforme) cohort includes ~170 patients rich clinical annotations: transcriptional subtype (Classical, Mesenchymal, Proneural, Neural), IDH mutation status, MGMT methylation, age, survival. multiple clinical variables make ideal column annotation bars, known molecular subtypes produce distinctive gene module patterns unsupervised clustering. Data structure: SummarizedExperiment users able : Construct SummarizedExperiment expression matrix sample metadata Select variable genes Row-scale expression matrix (z-score min-max normalization) Perform hierarchical clustering rows (genes) cut k assign gene modules Generate genes × samples heatmap row dendrogram, column annotation bars clinical metadata, gene module color strip Export scaled expression matrix gene module assignments TSV files Key parameters: n_top, scale_method (zscore/minmax/none), gene_k, column_split_by (metadata column) CLI outputs: scaled_expression.tsv, gene_modules.tsv Shiny inputs: n_top slider, scale method dropdown, gene_k slider, metadata column selector annotation, heatmap updates reactively Dependencies: SummarizedExperiment, ComplexHeatmap (Suggests), curatedTCGAData (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_se.R BiocManager::install(c("curatedTCGAData", "TCGAutils")) library(curatedTCGAData) library(TCGAutils) library(SummarizedExperiment)  # Download GBM RNA-seq data gbm <- curatedTCGAData("GBM", "RNASeq2*", version = "2.0.1",                         dry.run = FALSE) rse <- experiments(gbm)[[1]]  # Subset to top 2000 variable genes vars <- apply(assay(rse), 1, var, na.rm = TRUE) keep_genes <- names(sort(vars, decreasing = TRUE))[1:2000] rse <- rse[keep_genes, ]  # Get clinical data clin <- colData(gbm)  example_se <- SummarizedExperiment(   assays = list(exprs = assay(rse)),   colData = DataFrame(     sample_id = colnames(rse),     subtype   = clin[colnames(rse), "subtype_Transcriptome.Subtype"],     age       = clin[colnames(rse), "years_to_birth"]   ) )  usethis::use_data(example_se, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: “many principal components keep?” fundamental question single-cell workflows (Seurat ElbowPlot step). broken-stick model, Kaiser criterion, elbow heuristic encode different assumptions. Building tools compare teaches critical thinking method selection.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: 10X Genomics PBMC 3k dataset (~2,700 peripheral blood mononuclear cells) canonical Seurat tutorial dataset. contains ~10 immune cell types (T cells, B cells, NK cells, monocytes, dendritic cells), “right” number PCs actively debated: Seurat defaults 20, intrinsic dimensionality ~10 cell types may closer 10–15. makes estimation question genuinely interesting. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Select variable genes Run PCA extract eigenvalues (variance per component, cumulative variance) Apply multiple dimensionality estimation methods (broken-stick model, Kaiser criterion, elbow heuristic) report recommended number components Visualize scree plot overlays estimation method (broken-stick curve, Kaiser line, elbow annotation) Export eigenvalue tables dimensionality estimates TSV files Key parameters: n_top, methods (broken_stick/kaiser/elbow), max_pcs CLI outputs: eigenvalues.tsv, dimension_estimates.tsv Shiny inputs: n_top slider, checkboxes toggle overlay method, max_pcs slider, summary table recommendations Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, TENxPBMCData (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("TENxPBMCData") library(TENxPBMCData) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- TENxPBMCData("pbmc3k") example_sce <- sce  # Minimal colData — no pre-computed labels; that's fine for dim estimation colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, "Barcode", drop = FALSE]  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: Batch effects — systematic technical variation processing date, lane, operator — can dominate biological signal. Quantifying much variance PC attributes batch (via linear regression R²) standard diagnostic deciding whether correction needed.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: scRNAseq package provides Grun et al. (2016) dataset: ~1,700 human pancreatic cells profiled across multiple donors using CEL-Seq. combination donor (batch) cell type (biology) creates realistic scenario students must distinguish technical biological variation PCA space. PCA colored donor vs. cell type reveals batch effect immediately. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Select variable genes Quantify batch effects fitting linear model PC batch variable extracting R² values Apply simple batch correction (e.g., median-centering per batch) return corrected object Visualize dual-panel PCA plots (correction) colored batch, plus R² bar chart showing proportion variance attributable batch per PC Export batch variance quantification corrected counts TSV files Key parameters: batch_column, n_top, correct (logical), bio_column CLI outputs: batch_variance.tsv, corrected_counts.tsv Shiny inputs: Batch column dropdown, biological column dropdown, n_top slider, correction toggle, /PCA panels, R² bar chart Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- GrunPancreasData() example_sce <- sce  assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, c("donor", "sample")] colnames(colData(example_sce)) <- c("batch", "cell_type")  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Rationale: cells fall distinct types, identifying genes best distinguish type rest fundamental — core cell-type annotation. One-vs-rest testing produces ranked marker lists; dot plot (size = detection rate, color = mean expression) standard single-cell visualization.

title: Project Selection Guide
text: Baron et al. (2016) mouse pancreatic islet dataset contains ~1,886 cells across 9 clearly defined cell types: alpha, beta, delta, gamma (PP), acinar, ductal, stellate (activated quiescent), immune, Schwann cells. cell type well-known marker genes (e.g., Ins1/Ins2 beta cells, Gcg alpha cells), enabling students validate markers published biology. Note: mouse version — Project 4 uses human dataset study, code directly shared. Data structure: SingleCellExperiment users able : Construct SingleCellExperiment counts matrix cell metadata Filter low-expression genes Perform one-vs-rest statistical testing per cell type: compute log2 fold changes, Wilcoxon p-values, BH-adjusted p-values, rank genes per group Summarize top N markers per group compute overlap counts groups Visualize markers dot plot: genes y-axis, groups x-axis, dot size encodes detection rate, dot color encodes mean expression Export marker gene lists summaries TSV files Key parameters: group_column, n_markers, fc_threshold, detection_threshold CLI outputs: marker_genes.tsv, marker_summary.tsv Shiny inputs: Grouping variable dropdown, n_markers slider, FC threshold slider, detection threshold slider, dot plot + filterable gene table Dependencies: SingleCellExperiment, scRNAseq (data )
code: ## data-raw/example_sce.R BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") library(scRNAseq) library(SingleCellExperiment)  sce <- BaronPancreasData("mouse") example_sce <- sce  assays(example_sce) <- list(counts = counts(example_sce)) colData(example_sce) <- colData(example_sce)[, "label", drop = FALSE] colnames(colData(example_sce)) <- "cell_type"  usethis::use_data(example_sce, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Project Selection Guide
text: welcome propose project similar scope. approved, proposal must include: One paragraph describing biological analytical rationale Bioconductor dataset data-raw/ script showing prepare example data capability list describing users able package (4–6 bullet points) least one non-trivial visualization (just base R plot() call) CLI outputs — TSV files produced? Shiny inputs — user able adjust? Submit proposal GitHub Issue email starting implementation. instructor respond within 48 hours approval suggested modifications. Ground rules custom projects: Must operate SummarizedExperiment SingleCellExperiment input Must include constructor function entry point Must use real human mouse Bioconductor dataset (simulated data); RNA-seq scRNA-seq preferred (avoid microarray datasets) Must produce least one ggplot-based visualization Must duplicate existing project list Scope comparable — roughly 4–6 exported functions, full pipeline 15 steps

title: ADS 8192 - Developing Scientific Applications (Course Materials)
text: Unit 1 teaches graduate students build complete, reproducible scientific software R — raw analysis functions fully packaged, documented, deployable application. unit organized around core software engineering principles apply language domain: Separation concerns. Write analysis logic small, testable, composable R functions. add thin presentation layers — R API, Shiny web app, command-line interface — delegate functions without duplicating logic. layered-architecture idea ’ll find well-engineered software everywhere: keep (computation) separate (delivery users). Different audiences — scientists want point--click exploration, developers want composable functions, bioinformatics cores want scriptable CLI tools — benefit tested core.

title: ADS 8192 - Developing Scientific Applications (Course Materials)
text: completing Unit 1, students able : Design small, testable, composable functions operate robust data containers (SummarizedExperiment / SingleCellExperiment) return well-defined types — making easy reason , test, reuse. Separate analysis logic presentation logic. Core computation lives exported R functions; Shiny, CLI, scripting layers thin wrappers delegate core. prevents copy-paste drift (DRY) keeps every interface consistent. Build software multiple audiences. Scientists want point--click exploration (Shiny); package/pipeline developers want composable R functions can call programmatically; bioinformatics cores want non-interactive CLI tools slot automated workflows. One codebase serve . Package R code distribution. Turn loose scripts valid, installable R package using devtools / usethis workflows others can install_github() immediately use work. Pave road submission CRAN Bioconductor publication. Document thoroughly. Every exported function gets roxygen2 documentation; package ships pkgdown site; README vignette provide onboarding new users. Test meaningfully. testthat (edition 3) tests cover expected behavior informative error cases, giving confidence changes don’t silently break results. Think user experience. Shiny apps include input validation friendly messages; CLIs print help text produce standard file formats (TSV); error messages tell user went wrong . Practice reproducibility collaboration. Version control Git/GitHub, CI/CD via GitHub Actions, reproducible data preparation (data-raw/ scripts), tagged releases make work shareable auditable.

what: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
title: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
text: Constructs SummarizedExperiment counts matrix sample metadata data.frame. Optionally includes row (gene/feature) metadata.

title: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
code: make_se(counts, col_data, row_data = NULL)

title: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
text: counts matrix counts (genes x samples). Row names gene identifiers, column names sample identifiers. matrix, coerced via .matrix(). col_data data.frame sample metadata. Row names must match column names counts. row_data Optional data.frame gene/feature metadata. Row names must match row names counts. Default: NULL.

title: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
text: SummarizedExperiment object one assay named "counts".

title: Create a SummarizedExperiment from counts and metadata — make_se
code: counts <- matrix(rpois(100, 50), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene", 1:10) colnames(counts) <- paste0("sample", 1:10) meta <- data.frame(   treatment = rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 5),   row.names = colnames(counts) ) se <- make_se(counts, meta) se #> class: SummarizedExperiment  #> dim: 10 10  #> metadata(0): #> assays(1): counts #> rownames(10): gene1 gene2 ... gene9 gene10 #> rowData names(0): #> colnames(10): sample1 sample2 ... sample9 sample10 #> colData names(1): treatment

what: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
title: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
text: Extracts percentage variance explained principal component output run_pca.

title: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
code: pca_variance_explained(pca_result)

title: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
text: pca_result Output run_pca().

title: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
text: data.frame columns: PC Character, e.g. "PC1", "PC2". variance_percent Numeric, percentage total variance explained.

title: Get variance explained by each PC — pca_variance_explained
code: data(airway, package = "airway") result <- run_pca(airway, n_top = 50) var_df <- pca_variance_explained(result) head(var_df) #>    PC variance_percent #> 1 PC1        49.168360 #> 2 PC2        27.958056 #> 3 PC3        11.167149 #> 4 PC4         6.293835 #> 5 PC5         2.284729 #> 6 PC6         2.114628

what: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
title: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
text: Produces ggplot2 scatter plot PCA scores, optional color shape aesthetics mapped sample metadata columns.

title: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
code: plot_pca(   pca_result,   color_by = NULL,   shape_by = NULL,   pcs = c(1, 2),   point_size = 4 )

title: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
text: pca_result Output run_pca(). color_by Column name colData map point color. Default: NULL (color mapping). shape_by Column name colData map point shape. Default: NULL (shape mapping). pcs Integer vector length 2 specifying PCs plot. Default: c(1, 2). point_size Numeric point size. Default: 4.

title: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
text: ggplot object.

title: Create a PCA scatter plot — plot_pca
code: data(airway, package = "airway") result <- run_pca(airway, n_top = 50) plot_pca(result, color_by = "dex")

what: Run the PCA Explorer Shiny Application — run_app
title: Run the PCA Explorer Shiny Application — run_app
text: Launches interactive Shiny application exploring PCA results SummarizedExperiment data. app uses airway dataset default wraps package's core analysis functions.

title: Run the PCA Explorer Shiny Application — run_app
code: run_app(...)

text: ... Additional arguments passed shinyApp().

what: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca
title: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca
text: Performs principal component analysis SummarizedExperiment, first selecting top variable features, optionally log-transforming scaling.

title: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca
code: run_pca(   se,   assay_name = "counts",   n_top = 500,   scale = TRUE,   log_transform = TRUE )

title: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca
text: se SummarizedExperiment object. assay_name Name assay use. Default: "counts". n_top Number top variable features PCA. Default: 500. scale Logical; features scaled? Default: TRUE. log_transform Logical; counts log2-transformed (pseudocount 1)? Default: TRUE.

title: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca
text: list two elements: pca prcomp result object. scores data.frame PC scores merged sample metadata colData(se).

title: Run PCA on a SummarizedExperiment — run_pca

what: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
title: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
text: Writes PCA scores variance explained tab-separated files. Optionally saves PCA plot PNG.

title: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
code: save_pca_results(pca_result, output_dir, prefix = "pca")

title: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
text: pca_result Output run_pca(). output_dir Directory save files. Created exist. prefix Prefix filenames. Default: "pca".

title: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
text: Invisible NULL; files written output_dir: {prefix}_scores.tsv PCA scores sample metadata. {prefix}_variance.tsv Variance explained PC.

title: Save PCA results to files — save_pca_results
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(airway, package = "airway") result <- run_pca(airway, n_top = 50) save_pca_results(result, tempdir()) } # }

title: Select top variable features — top_variable_features
text: Subsets SummarizedExperiment n variable features (genes), ranked row variance.

title: Select top variable features — top_variable_features
code: top_variable_features(se, n = 500, assay_name = "counts")

title: Select top variable features — top_variable_features
text: se SummarizedExperiment object. n Number top variable features select. Default: 500. assay_name Name assay use. Default: "counts".

title: Select top variable features — top_variable_features
text: SummarizedExperiment subset top n variable features, preserving sample metadata.

title: Select top variable features — top_variable_features
code: data(airway, package = "airway") se_top <- top_variable_features(airway, n = 50) dim(se_top) #> [1] 50  8
